title: Cat Compulsive Tail Chasing and Attacking: Triage and Help
meta_desc: Is your cat attacking its tail? Learn about Feline Hyperesthesia, when to see a vet, and how a video of the behavior helps with diagnosis.
content_html:
What is Compulsive Tail Chasing?

While occasional play is normal, compulsive tail chasing and attacking occur when a cat becomes fixated on their tail, often growling, hissing, or biting it aggressively. This behavior is frequently associated with Feline Hyperesthesia Syndrome (FHS), a condition where the skin and nerves become hypersensitive, or it may stem from neuropathic pain, skin allergies, or high levels of anxiety.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this behavior is Medium. While it is usually not an immediate life-threatening crisis, it requires a veterinary consultation in the near future. However, it becomes an urgent situation if your cat has caused physical trauma to the tail, such as bleeding, open sores, or loss of fur, as these can lead to serious infections or permanent nerve damage.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Because cats often hide their symptoms or act differently in a clinical setting, capturing a video of the behavior at home is one of the most valuable tools for your veterinarian. A video allows the vet to see the intensity of the episodes and check for signs like skin rippling or dilated pupils. Additionally, clear photos of any specific wounds on the tail can help the medical team assess the severity of self-mutilation before you even reach the clinic.

faqs.question: Can stress cause a cat to attack its tail?
faqs.answer: Yes, chronic stress or a sudden change in the environment can trigger compulsive behaviors like tail chasing as a way for the cat to displace their anxiety.

faqs.question: Could my cat have an injury I can't see?
faqs.answer: It is possible. Nerve pain, impacted anal glands, or a previous tail fracture can cause a cat to lash out at the area in discomfort.

faqs.question: Is tail attacking a sign of fleas?
faqs.answer: Flea allergy dermatitis is a very common cause of skin sensitivity near the base of the tail, which can lead to frantic biting and chasing.
